message:
Colorado State Trooper Arrested by Loveland Police
 
On Monday, June 15, 2026, the Loveland Police Department received a report from a victim who disclosed allegations of sexual assault dating back to 2024. With the victim’s assistance, investigators identified 34-year-old Jeremiah Jon Patterson as the suspect. Patterson is a Colorado State Trooper. The victim and Patterson know each other, and the reported crimes occurred while Patterson was off duty.
 
On Friday evening, June 19, investigators received information suggesting Patterson might travel to Loveland to locate the victim. Using investigative techniques, including Flock Safety camera technology, Loveland Police Officers quickly located Patterson and conducted a high-risk traffic stop based on probable cause. Patterson was taken into custody and subsequently booked into jail after being interviewed.
 
Patterson was booked into the Larimer County Jail on the following charge(s):

▪️CRS 18-3-402(1)(4)(a) Sexual Assault, Strongarm by Force (F3)
▪️CRS 18-3-203(1)(i) 2nd Degree Assault, Strangulation (F4)
▪️Additional enhancement charges also applied
 
A booking photo may be obtained through the Records Division of the Larimer County Sheriff’s Office. Over the weekend, Magistrate Ryan set bond at $25,000 cash surety, with pre-trial restrictions.
 
“I truly commend the tremendous courage of the victim to come forward and disclose this nearly two-year-old sexual assault allegation,” Chief Doran said. “I praise our investigators for their thorough work, and I appreciate the District Attorney’s partnership, along with support from the Colorado State Patrol.”
 
If anyone knows of information regarding similar incidents involving Patterson, we ask them to contact the Loveland Police Department Tip Line at (970) 962-2032 or Larimer County Crime Stoppers at (970) 221-6868.

message:
A Virginia engineer has pleaded not guilty after being charged with destroying 13 Flock Safety AI-powered license plate reader cameras in Suffolk between April and October 2025.

Jeffrey Scott Sovern, 41, faces multiple felony counts of destruction of property. Prosecutors say he intentionally vandalized the cameras and stole components. Sovern has not denied the acts in public statements but argues that the growing network of AI surveillance cameras violates constitutional rights and is creating what he calls an “unhealthy surveillance state.”

Flock cameras use artificial intelligence to automatically capture and catalog license plates, vehicle types, colors, and other details. The data is stored in searchable databases that law enforcement agencies can access. Supporters say the technology helps solve crimes and locate missing people. Critics warn it enables mass tracking of ordinary citizens’ movements without warrants.

While some courts have acknowledged potential privacy concerns with expansive surveillance systems, most have so far permitted their use. The case has reignited a larger national conversation: Where should society draw the line between public safety and personal freedom?

At what point does a tool meant to protect the public begin to change the relationship between citizens and the state?

message:
As AI-powered cameras sweep across the U.S., one engineer's arrest for destroying surveillance devices exposes the deep public battle between crime fighting and personal privacy.

Jeffrey Scott Sovern, a 41-year-old Virginia engineer, has pleaded not guilty to 13 felony charges after prosecutors accused him of systematically dismantling and damaging Flock Safety license plate reader cameras.

 Operating between April and October 2025, Sovern allegedly targeted the AI-powered devices, which automatically capture and catalog license plates, vehicle models, and colors into databases accessible by law enforcement. While authorities view his actions as clear-cut vandalism, Sovern and his supporters argue that the expanding, unregulated network of surveillance technology violates constitutional rights and establishes an "unhealthy surveillance state" that tracks ordinary citizens without their consent.

The clash highlights a broader national reckoning over the boundaries of security and civil liberties. Proponents of Flock Safety cameras contend that real-time AI tracking is an essential tool for solving crimes, locating missing persons, and recovering stolen vehicles. However, privacy advocates warn that these systems normalize pervasive public monitoring, replacing foundational social solutions with authoritarian control. While courts have occasionally noted future constitutional concerns regarding mass tracking, the widespread adoption of AI surveillance continues to outpace legal boundaries, leaving citizens to grapple with where safety ends and the erosion of freedom begins.

source: Walsh, M. (2026, February 26). Flock cameras are getting destroyed, police using data to hunt vandals. Cybernews.

message: Kimberly and her husband Edward have been listed as missing from Hope AR since 11/2025. The last flock camera to pick up their license plate was in the OKC area back in March of 2026. Kimberly and her husband Edward's safety is top priority, if you have any information regarding Kimberly and/or Edward please call Hope Police Department.

message:
A Virginia engineer, Jeffrey Scott Sovern, has been charged with 13 counts of felony destruction of property after allegedly destroying 13 AI-powered license plate reader cameras operated by  Flock Safety⁠￼ in Suffolk, Virginia. He has pleaded not guilty. Investigators say components from several cameras, including solar panels, were found during a search of his home.  

Sovern argues that automated license plate reader (ALPR) systems are unconstitutional and contribute to what he calls an “unhealthy surveillance state.”

Sovern argues that automated license plate reader (ALPR) systems are unconstitutional and contribute to what he calls an “unhealthy surveillance state.”
